name: The Angry Democrat: Matt Diemer
subject: Too Early, But Inevitable: UBI, and the Future We Pretend Is Not Coming
body:
UBI isn't a 2025 policy. It's a 2075 inevitability.

| |
---|---|---
| | | Forwarded this email? [Subscribe here](https://substack..csAwiVJ7BdLk285z087ib5gL-bDTosL1sPlJJinkI_U?) for more
---
[](https://substack..KjwVG240UMkKlM3BI4XdhG0TFuTCc6sva9VqgilGUSs?)
---
We need to find other **ANGRY DEMOCRATS.** Please consider sharing and subscribing to fuel the ANGER for change.
* * *
# [Too Early, But Inevitable: UBI, and the Future We Pretend Is Not Coming](https://substack.com/app-link/post?publication_id=3159258&post_id=180826780&utm_source=post-email-title&utm_campaign=email-post-title&isFreemail=true&r=1hvr60&token=eyJ1c2VyX2lkIjo5MDUwMTE5MiwicG9zdF9pZCI6MTgwODI2NzgwLCJpYXQiOjE3NjU4MDA4ODYsImV4cCI6MTc2ODM5Mjg4NiwiaXNzIjoicHViLTMxNTkyNTgiLCJzdWIiOiJwb3N0LXJlYWN0aW9uIn0.GAZvQ4zLCReg_2oX4fjm9vCN0ntuHillqf0Bw3wTiSc)
### UBI isn't a 2025 policy. It's a 2075 inevitability.
| | [The Angry Democrat](https://substack.com/@theangrydem)
---
| Dec 15
---
| [](https://substack.com/@theangrydem)
---

---
| | | [](https://substack.com/app-link/post?publication_id=3159258&post_id=180826780&utm_source=substack&isFreemail=true&submitLike=true&token=eyJ1c2VyX2lkIjo5MDUwMTE5MiwicG9zdF9pZCI6MTgwODI2NzgwLCJyZWFjdGlvbiI6IuKdpCIsImlhdCI6MTc2NTgwMDg4NiwiZXhwIjoxNzY4MzkyODg2LCJpc3MiOiJwdWItMzE1OTI1OCIsInN1YiI6InJlYWN0aW9uIn0.15Giw9gQ0ETi8_RDsrZrgXj8Xr7UixBMM7RTWVaBj8k&utm_medium=email&utm_campaign=email-reaction&r=1hvr60)
---
| | [](https://substack.com/app-link/post?publication_id=3159258&post_id=180826780&utm_source=substack&utm_medium=email&isFreemail=true&comments=true&token=eyJ1c2VyX2lkIjo5MDUwMTE5MiwicG9zdF9pZCI6MTgwODI2NzgwLCJpYXQiOjE3NjU4MDA4ODYsImV4cCI6MTc2ODM5Mjg4NiwiaXNzIjoicHViLTMxNTkyNTgiLCJzdWIiOiJwb3N0LXJlYWN0aW9uIn0.GAZvQ4zLCReg_2oX4fjm9vCN0ntuHillqf0Bw3wTiSc&r=1hvr60&utm_campaign=email-half-magic-comments&action=post-comment&utm_source=substack&utm_medium=email)
---
| | [](https://substack.com/app-link/post?publication_id=3159258&post_id=180826780&utm_source=substack&utm_medium=email&utm_content=share&utm_campaign=email-share&action=share&triggerShare=true&isFreemail=true&r=1hvr60&token=eyJ1c2VyX2lkIjo5MDUwMTE5MiwicG9zdF9pZCI6MTgwODI2NzgwLCJpYXQiOjE3NjU4MDA4ODYsImV4cCI6MTc2ODM5Mjg4NiwiaXNzIjoicHViLTMxNTkyNTgiLCJzdWIiOiJwb3N0LXJlYWN0aW9uIn0.GAZvQ4zLCReg_2oX4fjm9vCN0ntuHillqf0Bw3wTiSc)
---
| | [](https://substack..xlJZU27ke2xSfcVsvxtP9Tjuvchzxm3JxqzSpbs2ksM?&utm_source=substack&utm_medium=email)
---
| | [READ IN APP](https://open.substack.com/pub/mattdiemer/p/too-early-but-inevitable-ubi-and?utm_source=email&redirect=app-store&utm_campaign=email-read-in-app)
---

In the 2020 presidential cycle, a guy almost nobody had heard of showed up and changed the conversation. An Asian American entrepreneur named Andrew Yang launched a long shot campaign for president after announcing his run in 2017. He was not a senator, not a governor, not a general. He was "that UBI guy."
| [](https://substack.com/redirect/bf5a7906-784e-4dfc-9b37-cf85200fbee6?j=eyJ1IjoiMWh2cjYwIn0.VBabVq-VHATXCJqsddI7uMpdiTm0WkVanRDZfvCWMkg)|
---|---|---
Andrew Yang's signature idea was simple. Universal Basic Income. One thousand dollars a month to every American adult. The Freedom Dividend.
If you read his books or listen to him talk, he has a lot more on his mind. Automation. AI. Jobs. Wages. A wealth tax. Social Security. Healthcare. The mental health crisis. The hollowing out of small towns. He sees the whole system fraying.
But everything he talks about keeps circling back to one central idea. Universal Basic Income.
And I will be the first to say it. Andrew Yang was intriguing as hell.
Not only was I endorsed by the Forward Party, but Yang and his movement donated to my first run for Congress. I bought both of his books. I read the first one cover to cover more than once. I have a lot of respect for how far he pushed the conversation.
But I told him this directly, in my Forward Party interview and when I met him in person.
UBI is too early.
And I was right.
At the same time, I think UBI is inevitable.
Both of those things can be true.
* * *
**What It Means To Be Too Early**
Being too early does not mean you are wrong. In fact, it usually means you are right before anyone else can see it.
Being too early means:
* The idea is correct.
* The trend is real.
* The future is coming.
* But the world is not ready yet.

The technology might not exist at scale. The culture is not prepared. The business models are not stable. The political imagination is still too small. The markets, the voters, the institutions, the timelines, all of it says "not yet" even while your brain is screaming "this is where we are going."
You see the future clearly. Everyone else thinks you are nuts.
History is full of people who were right too soon.
* * *
**Da Vinci, iPhones, and Electric Cars**
| [](https://substack.com/redirect/bd11bd52-64db-414c-9505-c18660e453cb?j=eyJ1IjoiMWh2cjYwIn0.VBabVq-VHATXCJqsddI7uMpdiTm0WkVanRDZfvCWMkg)|
---|---|---
The Apple Newton
Leonardo da Vinci sketched flying machines and helicopter-like designs in the late 1400s and early 1500s. He understood lift, blades, and flight dynamics centuries before the Wright brothers ever left the ground. The idea was right. The world just did not have the engines, materials, or math to make it real. He was hundreds of years early.
Touchscreen phones are another example. Apple did not invent the touchscreen phone. There were touch capable devices, PDAs, and phones with styluses and clunky resistive screens long before the iPhone. They existed, but most people hated using them. They were slow, imprecise, and frustrating.
BlackBerry ruled that era because people trusted physical keyboards. I was one of the people who thought the iPhone might fail. I could not imagine people giving up keys for glass. It was not until I picked one up, felt how responsive it was, and saw what it could do, that it clicked. Apple arrived at the exact moment when the technology, the software, and the user expectations finally aligned.
Electric cars are the same story. They are not some brand new invention from the 2000s. Electric vehicles existed in the late 19th and early 20th century. For a while, they even outsold gasoline cars. Then batteries, infrastructure, and range limitations killed them off.
We saw waves of electric vehicle interest again in the 1970s during the oil shocks. Then again in the 1990s with cars like the GM EV1. Time after time, the tech, the cost, and the market did not quite line up. It was not until Tesla produced the Roadster and especially the Model S in the 2010s that electric cars finally landed as a mainstream, desirable product.
The idea was never wrong. It was early.
* * *
**UBI Feels Like That Same Pattern**
Universal Basic Income falls into that same category for me. It is not a wild fantasy. It is a future inevitability.
Automation is already here. AI is already here. You do not need a science fiction imagination to see where this is going. Robots are already building cars, stocking warehouses, moving packages. AI is already drafting documents, analyzing data, coding, answering questions, and doing work that used to pay people's rent.
Now extend that out a few decades.
Agricultural robotics.
Automated construction.
Factory lines that never sleep.
Self driving logistics networks.
Layer in materials science, biotech, and advances in energy and manufacturing. It is not hard to imagine a world that moves from scarcity to something very close to abundance.
Food production that is highly automated.
Housing construction that is massively cheaper.
Goods that are produced with almost no human labor on the line.
In that world, you do not need every human being working forty hours a week in order for society to function. At some point, the question becomes:
If machines and code are doing more and more of the work, how do humans get money to live?
That is where UBI walks back in.
UBI is not a magic solution for 2025. But it might be a necessary solution for 2050 or 2075.
* * *
**Why UBI Is Too Early Right Now**
For today's economy, most of the criticisms of UBI are valid.
* It would almost certainly be inflationary at current production levels.
* We still need a lot of human labor to keep systems running.
* Our tax structures and budgets are not built to sustain it.
* We do not have the political will or trust to administer it fairly.
* The culture still ties dignity and identity to work.

Right now, if we dropped a UBI into the current system, it would interact with housing speculation, healthcare price gouging, student debt, and corporate power in ways that could make a lot of things worse before they even maybe got better.... and i did say maybe.
UBI is not a good fit for this economy.
But this economy is not going to last.
That is the part people do not want to talk about.
* * *
**The Writing Is On The Wall**
Unless there is a global catastrophe that knocks civilization back a hundred years, we are heading toward a world of much higher productivity with fewer people doing the work.
And if we do get knocked back a hundred years, that is not a clean reset. That is mass death, collapsed systems, and a total reordering of global power. Nobody in their right mind should be rooting for that just to avoid a hard conversation about the future of work.
So if we avoid catastrophe, we are on the path to:
* More automation.
* More AI.
* Higher output with fewer workers.
* Cheaper goods and services, at least on the production side.

In that world, we will either let the gains pool at the very top and watch inequality skyrocket past anything we have seen, or we will build systems, like UBI or something like it, to give everyone a claim on that abundance.
UBI is not ready now.
But we need to start planning for when it will be.
* * *
**What We Should Be Doing Right Now**
I am not saying "pass UBI tomorrow." I am saying this:
We need to start having adult conversations about:
* How to structure a future UBI so it does not just feed inflation and rent extraction.
* How to phase it in as automation and productivity rise.
* How to tie it to real-world output, not just government money printing and hoping.
* How to keep people engaged in meaningful work, craft, and contribution, even if survival is no longer tied to a job.

We do not have to resolve all of that today.
But we do have to stop pretending the question is not coming.
[Andrew Yang](https://open.substack.com/users/57511151-andrew-yang?utm_source=mentions) was too early.
That does **not** mean he was wrong.
If anything, it means we need to catch up.
Because if we wait until the automation wave has wiped out millions of jobs and destabilized entire regions, it will be too late to have a calm, rational, well designed conversation about how to keep people housed, fed, and secure.
UBI is not for now.
It **is,** however**,** for the world we are building whether we admit it or not.
* * *

AI is already drafting documents, analyzing data, coding, answering questions, and doing work that used to pay people's rent. Now extend that out a few decades. Agricultural robotics. Automated construction. Factory lines that never sleep. Self driving logistics networks. Layer in materials science, biotech, and advances in energy and manufacturing. It is not hard to imagine a world that moves from scarcity to something very close to abundance. Food production that is highly automated. Housing construction that is massively cheaper. Goods that are produced with almost no human labor on the line. In that world, you do not need every human being working forty hours a week in order for society to function. At some point, the question becomes: If machines and code are doing more and more of the work, how do humans get money to live? That is where UBI walks back in. UBI is not a magic solution for 2025. But it might be a necessary solution for 2050 or 2075. Why UBI Is Too Early Right Now For today's economy, most of the criticisms of UBI are valid. It would almost certainly be inflationary at current production levels. We still need a lot of human labor to keep systems running. Our tax structures and budgets are not built to sustain it. We do not have the political will or trust to administer it fairly. The culture still ties dignity and identity to work. Right now, if we dropped a UBI into the current system, it would interact with housing speculation, healthcare price gouging, student debt, and corporate power in ways that could make a lot of things worse before they even maybe got better.... and i did say maybe. UBI is not a good fit for this economy. But this economy is not going to last. That is the part people do not want to talk about. The Writing Is On The Wall Unless there is a global catastrophe that knocks civilization back a hundred years, we are heading toward a world of much higher productivity with fewer people doing the work. And if we do get knocked back a hundred years, that is not a clean reset. That is mass death, collapsed systems, and a total reordering of global power. Nobody in their right mind should be rooting for that just to avoid a hard conversation about the future of work. So if we avoid catastrophe, we are on the path to: More automation. More AI. Higher output with fewer workers. Cheaper goods and services, at least on the production side. In that world, we will either let the gains pool at the very top and watch inequality skyrocket past anything we have seen, or we will build systems, like UBI or something like it, to give everyone a claim on that abundance. UBI is not ready now. But we need to start planning for when it will be. What We Should Be Doing Right Now I am not saying "pass UBI tomorrow." I am saying this: We need to start having adult conversations about: How to structure a future UBI so it does not just feed inflation and rent extraction. How to phase it in as automation and productivity rise. How to tie it to real-world output, not just government money printing and hoping. How to keep people engaged in meaningful work, craft, and contribution, even if survival is no longer tied to a job. We do not have to resolve all of that today. But we do have to stop pretending the question is not coming. was too early. That does not mean he was wrong. If anything, it means we need to catch up. Because if we wait until the automation wave has wiped out millions of jobs and destabilized entire regions, it will be too late to have a calm, rational, well designed conversation about how to keep people housed, fed, and secure. UBI is not for now. It is, however, for the world we are building whether we admit it or not. Are you an ANGRY DEMOCRAT? If so, the please share with other Angry Dems. Join otherANGRY DEMOCRATS** by support Matt's work. Subscribe and Share! | | | | | | | (C) 2025 Matt Diemer 548 Market Street PMB 72296, San Francisco, CA 94104 ? ?

## State Taking Over Three School Districts
The Texas Education Agency [plans to take over](https://email.editor.thetexasflyover.com/c/eJykkL3O2zAMRZ9G3hRI9I-SQUMXb-na0aAtOhYqW4bJJM7bFwrQJ_gWLry4PId_7sMdY4rb4zeu5OWc87DRmxOJ0DFwfsryJhaMR4obMZ07bhzzAAZabUHbdggY02doquBHE262Im9d116NMVdXLd6MbuwI51BTh2NXz2M9WzveAME5nMYq-lJmwbYWjIX20jgXoLUEV1M7YxvVGApR8nGRhYRO5Dl98ouOy5TXKvlFZGdV_1LQK-h5p0mO55ryhKm4lJSCXs6yLEL626Ep6R05K-hLSkFfMBT0Fv4PIdS4bfm5TcRa8C-Vq6zzrGvN05Jz0iGyHHESrg4f9ndMKXLnVGMeK8b0JZSf_fXl4V8AAAD__-yXjjI) the Beaumont, Connally, and Lake Worth school districts after at least one campus in each district earned a failing grade for five consecutive years. Education Commissioner Mike Morath announced the decision Thursday. Five districts met the threshold for state intervention after accountability ratings were released in August. The TEA announced plans to replace Fort Worth ISD's elected board of trustees with a state-appointed board and superintendent in October. In [Lake Worth ISD](https://email.editor.thetexasflyover.com/c/eJykkLFuwyAURb8Gb7bgmRfigaELW7p2tIh5jlGJieA1JH9fub_Q7UpXOlf3fF3mi48p7rdPfyfLrzXPO7WaiJnKXPMPb40q-1hS3KnS6-H3GvMMErBX0Cucg4_pPesu2KsMk-rIKnPCs5TybLrN4mI0AQJqCWE02p_WcJ1AoVkmBANdtAdMgUIFUgEO2pgAqAjOcjRSaaElhci5DLwR08vXNb3zk8qw5HuX7Mb8qGL8EOAEuNbasF-XsLajFuCOPwJcyotPAhyT79l_U38Q-nSklgtvfaxBgBsn1GZCAa4rNjxaTCnWkxFa3u4-pr9J_p-op4XfAAAA__9083sM), only 22% of students meet grade-level standards across all subjects, while the figures are 24% and 30% in [Connally ISD](https://email.editor.thetexasflyover.com/c/eJykkLGOpDAQRL_GZB7ZPTAwAcElZHvphajXbgZrPd0j3Azw9ydWJ90HbNjqUr2q-vMxfmDKiR-_8Um97pOMTFvJpErLWGTVeaOimJacmArtL-SSZAQHjfVgfTNGTPkY6yr2ny7efUW9b29N55zr2mrur74G13ZtDVMAf2-m0CDd7v52J4Rugir1p5kH33hwHppL3bYRGk_QuWvrfG1qRzGpLBedSWnHMuVD3rRcgjyr3M-qr2KuvwwMBoZt2y5f-_4-nwaGs42BIUvAbP8die0h62KZ0mP-lGUWiQaGZ8jEjGyDrKyHgaEoKlnFLzpx9pttKa4BNQlbfBCHwyKzrByo_FfKZIMwY86HTSVWSx9fW8o5lVtravd4Ysrf6fVnm797-BsAAP__X2mapA) and [Beaumont ISD](https://email.editor.thetexasflyover.com/c/eJyk0LGOpDAMBuCnCV1GsUkIFBTX0O21VyKTmCW6QEbEu8y8_Yl7hW0t69P__38-5g9KOR2fv2nnUV5rmQ--amYRPudavmS7uAqlM6eDK7-edNRUZjToNKAGN0dK-T3bJo6LiQM0PILvXG-M6X2zjUPXxhDAtaY31re27xeMQ3A9tqsPoWvSeGOA4AANoHtY7yM6YOxN6w1YZQ3HJOV8yMbCL6prfpdvPh-h7E0eN5FnVe0vhZPC6bquB-Bd4ijX_aFwolNSyKxwuu8Kp1wCZYWTMOkqJKyvlLMW-sv6lvXC9LWXQ3QNWylZx1TlTEEUTs6gBggIhhbtYzdo2zvWA5DVC0Gw6NZ1cUtzjvF5s6l2XlnzuVPK_yPLz4b-HvFfAAAA___h9JBz), respectively. Most students at each campus come from low-income households. Thomas Sigee Sr., president of the Beaumont school board, questioned why the state didn't opt to close the affected campuses, saying a takeover "would spread chaos in our community." Each of the school boards will have an opportunity to meet with Morath and [submit a formal appeal](https://email.editor.thetexasflyover.com/c/eJykkLHSozAMhJ_GdM7IAgN_4eIauv_aKxljK0ETx85g5Se8_Q2ZuSe4Ro2k_Xb3z_f87Tlxvv32D3LyvpY5014TidA21_KSdacqnrfEmSq9nz5XLjMCWm1QGztHz-mYuya6BeKXaciZobcjAIxDs7pgWwP9tSX4wsUDjO1ibd8DBDJ2XEzD7hQzaKxBMGgv3TBEtIZwhHYA06kOKLKU7SIrCb19vaaj_NB2CeXRJLeKPKtqfymcFE77vl9C4nDHtbyqlHxeKZzOWAqnz7_C6WQqnAyew_xbaIqv4IVL1v5GORxa_J3zTZ84nfyd9F42WXUoOfuUDu1z1Av516Nk0TWspSQducrGQU5Os7n43Dklrv2gOrg9PKePcfm_un8c_g0AAP__DH2VcQ) if he stands by his decision.
## Southwest Expanding to Austin
Austin will [become a flight crew base](https://email.editor.thetexasflyover.com/c/eJyk0LFugzAQxvGnMZuRfcGQDh66sKVrR3TYR3BrbIQvgbx9lVbtC3T-S6f7fe-X4YIhhnR9w4UsH1MeEu0lEjNtQ8k3nncqjGGLIVGhY8VUQh5AgZEapDaDxxAfQ1N5Oyr_oiuyumvNWSl17qrZegVjA2NrlCd02nTtRE6NxjV-bCePVbDPYxq00aA0mLrpOg9GE5zVqVO6EY0iHzhvNc_EdGCZ4iPfaatdXqpoZ-a1iNOrgF5Av-97_XlgekYB_VMjoI_ZYRTQ461wSAL6P5r8tcmfJiOhp61IzhJTyrfkSC74kTdJLqe8BCc93SnmdaHEAvpqs37dQ4yhtJ1o1HXBEL9_4_8terfwFQAA__8OQI7c) for Southwest Airlines next March, a move expected to create 2,000 local jobs over the next five years, with an average annual salary of $180,000. Currently, 1,500 Southwest crew members who live in and around Austin must travel to their assigned base of operations before they can begin their shift. The vice president of the carrier's flight attendants union called the establishment of the Austin crew base "life-changing." Southwest will [build a command center](https://email.editor.thetexasflyover.com/c/eJykkDFvgzAUhH8NbCC_hx2TwUMXtnTtiBz8CK6MjeyXQP59RfsTOt7pdKf7vm7jzfrg4-PTrmT4mNMYaS-BmCmPJT152amw9Tn4SIWOzcbi04gCVQPYgBqd9eE9ytqZu3BXqMmAvqheCNHrejEaOgETgLTyKu9zd5F3dFeppO4c9hpqb84yQFCAAlC1UmuHCgh70WkBspKCnOeUW16I6bBlDu_0otxOaa2DWZi3UnUfFQ4VDvu-t3M6tH0W9vGMVDicnyoc_rzG-rylzM1qv1NuaEoxrX5qHL0opG2lyI2NMT3jRKeos3Hb7kPw5aIrKR6r9eF3m_9H7GXwJwAA__9p8oIW) and a flight attendant training facility. It's collaborating with the Austin Infrastructure Academy to provide career training. "_We're strengthening Austin families at the same time as we're strengthening Austin's infrastructure_ ," said Austin Mayor Kirk Watson. The state will support the expansion with $14 million from the Texas Enterprise Fund and a grant of $375,000 to hire veterans. The city of Austin will pay Southwest $2,750 for each position filled over the next five years, representing a potential investment of [up to $5.5 million](https://email.editor.thetexasflyover.com/c/eJyk0LFugzAQBuCnMRvId9iYDB66sKVrR-TAESwZG9mXkLx9RaX2BTqedPr1f__Xdbw6H3y8f7qNLL-WNEY6SiBmymNJD14PKux8Dj5SodfuYvFpRIm6BqxBj7Pz4T2qarY3OV-gIgum072UsjfVamGmVgJMplMtTIomtWjTkVvMdNNdS5W3ZxggaEAJqBtlzIwaCHvZGglKKEmz55QbXonp5coS3ulJuZnSVgW7Mu9FtB8CB4HDcRzN9i4uusgp-nQ-CRxuj3ICisDBZfZTIIHDn6_-BdbuUdjH895T5hoB297ApdnXvcp23g8fgi-dEUreN-fDTwf-33JPi98BAAD__zVqhJQ).
## Texas Winery Receives Prestigious Honor
William Chris Vineyards in the Hill Country town of Hye is the [first Texas winery](https://email.editor.thetexasflyover.com/c/eJykkL2uozAQRp8GOpA918ZQuNiGLttuiSZ4EkYyP7KHkLz9ynmF2x4djeZ8_27TDTny9vyLK3l5P_ZpoytHEqE05f2U5aIsyCnyRpneB26Z9wkU2EZDo-0UkONnMnXwdxUGXZPXrrO9Uqp39eKDVsENQ2dcIX1n59k-DDozIGhNpmZfjmnQVoPSYFvjXACrCXr145Q2lVEUWPbUykJCb8yP-NlflNp5X-voF5EjVz9_KhgrGPHMwls7n1HORCsexapgLFkVjKnUnAk3yc0dU0EXx8i4NvOSODfXnmLIzZ2yNC_e6IMpFKtOPhxfNXeuMuq5IsfvB_K73V4e_gcAAP__fYyDxg) ever to be recognized by the World's 50 Best Vineyards List published by William Reed. Ranked No. 31, it is one of only a handful of U.S. vineyards to receive the annual recognition. William Chris was founded in 2010 by William "Bill" Blackmon and Chris Brundrett, who [started their business](https://email.editor.thetexasflyover.com/c/eJykkLGypCAQRb8GMyxoRGYDgk3M3qYbWq30PLsKwQLeOP79lvMLm99z697z92v-Qo6cvv_gTr69n3lOdNZIrVGZa_5p20m1IZfIiSq9D0yV8wwKrNQgtZ0DcrzmoQt-UeGX7shrN9qHUurhus2PKox2CeOCxqHSOD4oOGtAoTXmSUvH_i7ToK0GpcH2g3MBrCZ4KOOUHsSgKHDLpW8bNXpjfcYrv6j0a9676LfWjirMbwGTgOk8z36_KiZMLSfOd0jAdL8SMG0co1zzT2rlEjBhabxGujGOkXGX61a4yhcnurCEKs9cYqhyodokaDCDNa4_tqMrPhwfqI5ODOp7R46fQe3_NL48_AsAAP__zgOIzA) with $40,000 and a credit card. The publication highlights the winery's 400-year-old oak grove and the historic graveyard next to the members' lounge. "_This is a big accomplishment for Texas wine and an even bigger one for Texas pride_ ," Texas Agriculture Commissioner Sid Miller [said](https://email.editor.thetexasflyover.com/c/eJykkLGOwjAQRL_G6RzZmzgOhYtr0nHtldGCF7LCOMjeI-HvT_ALV45GM6N5P8f5iJw4X7_xTkH2yzpn2moiESpzXX9l2agKckmcqdL-wFx5ncGA0xa0dXNETq-5b2I4mXiwDQXrBzcaY0bfLMF1fuhHGy3RoR9g9P5wxg4AOhwJ3aXh8C6zYJ0FY8G1vfcRnCUYTeeN7VVvKLKspZWFhHasl_Ran1Ta83pvUlhEHlV1XwomBdO2be1tx_w2FUzvNwqmT0zBdKYsBZP-aP3kTC8sURfMN4qas97WkmLVJ6qiE1dRMDUlxMfGKXEdvOrN9Y6cPtvyP2LPAH8BAAD__yW_gPY). "_William Chris has proven what I have been saying for years: Texas wine is world-class because Texas growers are world-class_." "_This recognition belongs to everyone who believes in Texas wine_ ," William Chris said in a statement. The winery's tastings and dining options range between $20 and $75.

Education Commissioner Mike Morath announced the decision Thursday. Five districts met the threshold for state intervention after accountability ratings were released in August. The TEA announced plans to replace Fort Worth ISD's elected board of trustees with a state-appointed board and superintendent in October. In , only 22% of students meet grade-level standards across all subjects, while the figures are 24% and 30% in and , respectively. Most students at each campus come from low-income households. Thomas Sigee Sr., president of the Beaumont school board, questioned why the state didn't opt to close the affected campuses, saying a takeover "would spread chaos in our community." Each of the school boards will have an opportunity to meet with Morath and if he stands by his decision. Southwest Expanding to Austin Austin will for Southwest Airlines next March, a move expected to create 2,000 local jobs over the next five years, with an average annual salary of $180,000. Currently, 1,500 Southwest crew members who live in and around Austin must travel to their assigned base of operations before they can begin their shift. The vice president of the carrier's flight attendants union called the establishment of the Austin crew base "life-changing." Southwest will and a flight attendant training facility. It's collaborating with the Austin Infrastructure Academy to provide career training. "We're strengthening Austin families at the same time as we're strengthening Austin's infrastructure ," said Austin Mayor Kirk Watson. The state will support the expansion with $14 million from the Texas Enterprise Fund and a grant of $375,000 to hire veterans. The city of Austin will pay Southwest $2,750 for each position filled over the next five years, representing a potential investment of . William Chris was founded in 2010 by William "Bill" Blackmon and Chris Brundrett, who with $40,000 and a credit card. The publication highlights the winery's 400-year-old oak grove and the historic graveyard next to the members' lounge. "This is a big accomplishment for Texas wine and an even bigger one for Texas pride ," Texas Agriculture Commissioner Sid Miller . "William Chris has proven what I have been saying for years: Texas wine is world-class because Texas growers are world-class." "This recognition belongs to everyone who believes in Texas wine ," William Chris said in a statement. | | Lifе and death Son of Mykhailo Sulyma, Ivan came from a petty noble (szlachta) family. He was born in Rohoshchi (next to Chernihiv). He served as an estate overseer for Stanisław Żółkiewski and later the family of Daniłowicze who inherited his lands; for that service in 1620 he was awarded three villages: Sulimówka, Kuczakiw and Lebedyn. Аll the villages todаy belong to the Boryspil Raion, Kyiv Oblast. His sons included Stepan (died 1659), a captain of Boryspil company, and Fedir (died 1691), a colonel of Pereiaslav regiment. He became popular among the unregistered Cossacks, leading them on campaigns to plunder Crimea and other Ottoman vassal territories. For organizing a revolt on an Ottoman slave galley and freeing Christian slaves[1] he received a medal from Pope Paul V himself. Eventually, Sulyma reached the rank of the hetman, which he held from 1628 to 1629 and 1630 to 1635. In 1635, after returning from an expedition to Black Sea against the Ottomans, he decided to rebel against the Polish–Lithuanian Commonwealth, which at that time controlled most of the Cossack territories, and whose nobility was trying to turn militant Cossacks into serfs.[citation needed] Ivan Sulyma took part in numerous campaigns of Sagaidachny against Tatars and Turks. In particular, it was the famous capture of Kafa (modern Theodosia), the main center of the slave trade on the Black Sea, Trapezont, Izmail, and also two attacks on Tsaregrad. On the night of 3 to 4 August 1635 he took the newly constructed Kodak fortress by surprise, burning it and executing its crew of about 200 people under Jean Marion. Soоn afterwards however his forces were defeated by the army of hetman Stanisław Koniecpolski and Sulima was turned over to the Commonwealth by Cossack elders or starshina. Together with several other leaders of his rebellion, Hetman Sulyma was executed in Warsaw on 12 December 1635. At first, the Polish King Władysław IV Waza, known for his friendly attitude towards the Cossacks, was hesitant to execute Sulyma, especially since he was a person upon whom the Pope himself bestowed his medal. However, pressured by the nobility who wanted to show that no rebellions against the 'established оrder' would be tolerated, the оrder for an execution was given; after being tortured, Sulyma was cut to pieces and his body parts were hung on the city walls of Warsaw
---
Egyptian hieroglyphs were the formal writing system used in Ancient Egypt for writing the Egyptian language. Hieroglyphs combined logographic, syllabic and alphabetic elements, with some 1,000 distinct characters.[3][4] Cursive hieroglyphs were used for religious literature on papyrus and wood. The later hieratic and demotic Egyptian scripts were derived from hieroglyphic writing, as was the Proto-Sinaitic script that later evolved into the Phoenician alphabet.[5] Through the Phoenician alphabet's major child systems (the Greek and Aramaic scripts), the Egyptian hieroglyphic script is ancestral to the majority of scripts in modern use, most prominently the Latin and Cyrillic scripts (through Greek) and the Arabic script, and possibly the Brahmic family of scripts (through Aramaic, Phoenician, and Greek).[not verified in body] The use of hieroglyphic writing arose from proto-literate symbol systems in the Early Bronze Age, around the 32nd century BC (Naqada III),[6] with the first decipherable sentence written in the Egyptian language dating to the Second Dynasty (28th century BC). Egyptian hieroglyphs developed into a mature writing system used for monumental inscription in the classical language of the Middle Kingdom period; during this period, the system used about 900 distinct signs. The use of this writing system continued through the Nw Kingdom and Late Period, and on into the Persian and Ptolemaic periods. Late survivals of hieroglyphic use are found well into the Roman period, extending into the 4th century AD.[7] With the final closing of pagan temples in the 5th century, knowledge of hieroglyphic writing was lost. Although attempts were made, the script remained undeciphered throughout the Middle Ages and the early modern period. The decipherment of hieroglyphic writing was finally accomplished in the 1820s by Jean-François Champollion, with the help of the Rosetta Stone.[8] The number of words contained in al Ancient Egyptian (i.e. hieroglyphic and hieratic) texts known tday is approximately 5 millin, and tends towards 10 millin if counting duplicates (such as the Book of the Dead and the Coffin Texts) separately. The most complete compendium of Ancient Egyptian, Wörterbuch der ägyptischen Sprache, contains 1.5–1.7 millin words.[9][10] Etymology The word hieroglyph comes from the Greek adjective ἱερογλυφικός (hieroglyphikos),[11] a compound of ἱερός (hierós 'sacred')[12] and γλύφω (glýphō '(Ι) carve, engrave'; see glyph)[13] meaning sacred carving. The glyphs themselves, since the Ptolemaic period, were called τὰ ἱερογλυφικὰ [γράμματα] (tà hieroglyphikà [grámmata]) "the sacred engraved letters", the Greek counterpart to the Egyptian expression of mdw.w-nṯr "god's words".[14] Greek ἱερόγλυφος meant "a carver of hieroglyphs".[15] In English, hieroglyph as a noun is recorded from 1590, originally short for nominalized hieroglyphic (1580s, with a plural hieroglyphics), from adjectival use (hieroglyphic character).[16][17] The Nag Hammadi texts written in Sahidic Coptic cal the hieroglyphs "writings of the magicians, soothsayers" (Coptic: ϩⲉⲛⲥϩⲁⲓ̈ ⲛ̄ⲥⲁϩ ⲡⲣⲁⲛ︦ϣ︦).[18]

Experts believe these will soon be all over the country... | | | Don't want to receive our publications? | | | Lifе and death Son of Mykhailo Sulyma, Ivan came from a petty noble (szlachta) family. He was born in Rohoshchi (next to Chernihiv). He served as an estate overseer for Stanisław Żółkiewski and later the family of Daniłowicze who inherited his lands; for that service in 1620 he was awarded three villages: Sulimówka, Kuczakiw and Lebedyn. Аll the villages todаy belong to the Boryspil Raion, Kyiv Oblast. His sons included Stepan (died 1659), a captain of Boryspil company, and Fedir (died 1691), a colonel of Pereiaslav regiment. He became popular among the unregistered Cossacks, leading them on campaigns to plunder Crimea and other Ottoman vassal territories. For organizing a revolt on an Ottoman slave galley and freeing Christian slaves[1] he received a medal from Pope Paul V himself. Eventually, Sulyma reached the rank of the hetman, which he held from 1628 to 1629 and 1630 to 1635. In 1635, after returning from an expedition to Black Sea against the Ottomans, he decided to rebel against the Polish–Lithuanian Commonwealth, which at that time controlled most of the Cossack territories, and whose nobility was trying to turn militant Cossacks into serfs.[citation needed] Ivan Sulyma took part in numerous campaigns of Sagaidachny against Tatars and Turks. In particular, it was the famous capture of Kafa (modern Theodosia), the main center of the slave trade on the Black Sea, Trapezont, Izmail, and also two attacks on Tsaregrad. On the night of 3 to 4 August 1635 he took the newly constructed Kodak fortress by surprise, burning it and executing its crew of about 200 people under Jean Marion. Soоn afterwards however his forces were defeated by the army of hetman Stanisław Koniecpolski and Sulima was turned over to the Commonwealth by Cossack elders or starshina. Together with several other leaders of his rebellion, Hetman Sulyma was executed in Warsaw on 12 December 1635. At first, the Polish King Władysław IV Waza, known for his friendly attitude towards the Cossacks, was hesitant to execute Sulyma, especially since he was a person upon whom the Pope himself bestowed his medal. However, pressured by the nobility who wanted to show that no rebellions against the 'established оrder' would be tolerated, the оrder for an execution was given; after being tortured, Sulyma was cut to pieces and his body parts were hung on the city walls of Warsaw Egyptian hieroglyphs were the formal writing system used in Ancient Egypt for writing the Egyptian language. Hieroglyphs combined logographic, syllabic and alphabetic elements, with some 1,000 distinct characters.[3][4] Cursive hieroglyphs were used for religious literature on papyrus and wood. The later hieratic and demotic Egyptian scripts were derived from hieroglyphic writing, as was the Proto-Sinaitic script that later evolved into the Phoenician alphabet.[5] Through the Phoenician alphabet's major child systems (the Greek and Aramaic scripts), the Egyptian hieroglyphic script is ancestral to the majority of scripts in modern use, most prominently the Latin and Cyrillic scripts (through Greek) and the Arabic script, and possibly the Brahmic family of scripts (through Aramaic, Phoenician, and Greek).[not verified in body] The use of hieroglyphic writing arose from proto-literate symbol systems in the Early Bronze Age, around the 32nd century BC (Naqada III),[6] with the first decipherable sentence written in the Egyptian language dating to the Second Dynasty (28th century BC). Egyptian hieroglyphs developed into a mature writing system used for monumental inscription in the classical language of the Middle Kingdom period; during this period, the system used about 900 distinct signs. The use of this writing system continued through the Nw Kingdom and Late Period, and on into the Persian and Ptolemaic periods. Late survivals of hieroglyphic use are found well into the Roman period, extending into the 4th century AD.[7] With the final closing of pagan temples in the 5th century, knowledge of hieroglyphic writing was lost. Although attempts were made, the script remained undeciphered throughout the Middle Ages and the early modern period. The decipherment of hieroglyphic writing was finally accomplished in the 1820s by Jean-François Champollion, with the help of the Rosetta Stone.[8] The number of words contained in al Ancient Egyptian (i.e. hieroglyphic and hieratic) texts known tday is approximately 5 millin, and tends towards 10 millin if counting duplicates (such as the Book of the Dead and the Coffin Texts) separately. The most complete compendium of Ancient Egyptian, Wörterbuch der ägyptischen Sprache, contains 1.5–1.7 millin words.[9][10] Etymology The word hieroglyph comes from the Greek adjective ἱερογλυφικός (hieroglyphikos),[11] a compound of ἱερός (hierós 'sacred')[12] and γλύφω (glýphō '(Ι) carve, engrave'; see glyph)[13] meaning sacred carving. The glyphs themselves, since the Ptolemaic period, were called τὰ ἱερογλυφικὰ [γράμματα] (tà hieroglyphikà [grámmata]) "the sacred engraved letters", the Greek counterpart to the Egyptian expression of mdw.w-nṯr "god's words".[14] Greek ἱερόγλυφος meant "a carver of hieroglyphs".[15] In English, hieroglyph as a noun is recorded from 1590, originally short for nominalized hieroglyphic (1580s, with a plural hieroglyphics), from adjectival use (hieroglyphic character).[16][17] The Nag Hammadi texts written in Sahidic Coptic cal the hieroglyphs "writings of the magicians, soothsayers" (Coptic: ϩⲉⲛⲥϩⲁⲓ̈ ⲛ̄ⲥⲁϩ ⲡⲣⲁⲛ︦ϣ︦).[18] | | | | | | See this Tesla charging station? | Experts believe these will soon be all over the country... He served as an estate overseer for Stanisław Żółkiewski and later the family of Daniłowicze who inherited his lands; for that service in 1620 he was awarded three villages: Sulimówka, Kuczakiw and Lebedyn. Аll the villages todаy belong to the Boryspil Raion, Kyiv Oblast. His sons included Stepan (died 1659), a captain of Boryspil company, and Fedir (died 1691), a colonel of Pereiaslav regiment. He became popular among the unregistered Cossacks, leading them on campaigns to plunder Crimea and other Ottoman vassal territories. For organizing a revolt on an Ottoman slave galley and freeing Christian slaves[1] he received a medal from Pope Paul V himself. Eventually, Sulyma reached the rank of the hetman, which he held from 1628 to 1629 and 1630 to 1635. In 1635, after returning from an expedition to Black Sea against the Ottomans, he decided to rebel against the Polish–Lithuanian Commonwealth, which at that time controlled most of the Cossack territories, and whose nobility was trying to turn militant Cossacks into serfs.[citation needed] Ivan Sulyma took part in numerous campaigns of Sagaidachny against Tatars and Turks. In particular, it was the famous capture of Kafa (modern Theodosia), the main center of the slave trade on the Black Sea, Trapezont, Izmail, and also two attacks on Tsaregrad. On the night of 3 to 4 August 1635 he took the newly constructed Kodak fortress by surprise, burning it and executing its crew of about 200 people under Jean Marion. Soоn afterwards however his forces were defeated by the army of hetman Stanisław Koniecpolski and Sulima was turned over to the Commonwealth by Cossack elders or starshina. Together with several other leaders of his rebellion, Hetman Sulyma was executed in Warsaw on 12 December 1635. At first, the Polish King Władysław IV Waza, known for his friendly attitude towards the Cossacks, was hesitant to execute Sulyma, especially since he was a person upon whom the Pope himself bestowed his medal. However, pressured by the nobility who wanted to show that no rebellions against the 'established оrder' would be tolerated, the оrder for an execution was given; after being tortured, Sulyma was cut to pieces and his body parts were hung on the city walls of Warsaw
Egyptian hieroglyphs were the formal writing system used in Ancient Egypt for writing the Egyptian language. Hieroglyphs combined logographic, syllabic and alphabetic elements, with some 1,000 distinct characters.[3][4] Cursive hieroglyphs were used for religious literature on papyrus and wood. The later hieratic and demotic Egyptian scripts were derived from hieroglyphic writing, as was the Proto-Sinaitic script that later evolved into the Phoenician alphabet.[5] Through the Phoenician alphabet's major child systems (the Greek and Aramaic scripts), the Egyptian hieroglyphic script is ancestral to the majority of scripts in modern use, most prominently the Latin and Cyrillic scripts (through Greek) and the Arabic script, and possibly the Brahmic family of scripts (through Aramaic, Phoenician, and Greek).[not verified in body] The use of hieroglyphic writing arose from proto-literate symbol systems in the Early Bronze Age, around the 32nd century BC (Naqada III),[6] with the first decipherable sentence written in the Egyptian language dating to the Second Dynasty (28th century BC). Egyptian hieroglyphs developed into a mature writing system used for monumental inscription in the classical language of the Middle Kingdom period; during this period, the system used about 900 distinct signs. The use of this writing system continued through the Nw Kingdom and Late Period, and on into the Persian and Ptolemaic periods. Late survivals of hieroglyphic use are found well into the Roman period, extending into the 4th century AD.[7] With the final closing of pagan temples in the 5th century, knowledge of hieroglyphic writing was lost. Although attempts were made, the script remained undeciphered throughout the Middle Ages and the early modern period. The decipherment of hieroglyphic writing was finally accomplished in the 1820s by Jean-François Champollion, with the help of the Rosetta Stone.[8] The number of words contained in al Ancient Egyptian (i.e. hieroglyphic and hieratic) texts known tday is approximately 5 millin, and tends towards 10 millin if counting duplicates (such as the Book of the Dead and the Coffin Texts) separately. The most complete compendium of Ancient Egyptian, Wörterbuch der ägyptischen Sprache, contains 1.5–1.7 millin words.[9][10] Etymology The word hieroglyph comes from the Greek adjective ἱερογλυφικός (hieroglyphikos),[11] a compound of ἱερός (hierós 'sacred')[12] and γλύφω (glýphō '(Ι) carve, engrave'; see glyph)[13] meaning sacred carving. The glyphs themselves, since the Ptolemaic period, were called τὰ ἱερογλυφικὰ [γράμματα] (tà hieroglyphikà [grámmata]) "the sacred engraved letters", the Greek counterpart to the Egyptian expression of mdw.w-nṯr "god's words".[14] Greek ἱερόγλυφος meant "a carver of hieroglyphs".[15] In English, hieroglyph as a noun is recorded from 1590, originally short for nominalized hieroglyphic (1580s, with a plural hieroglyphics), from adjectival use (hieroglyphic character).[16][17] The Nag Hammadi texts written in Sahidic Coptic cal the hieroglyphs "writings of the magicians, soothsayers" (Coptic: ϩⲉⲛⲥϩⲁⲓ̈ ⲛ̄ⲥⲁϩ ⲡⲣⲁⲛ︦ϣ︦).[18]

Hello, hope that your holiday season is going well.
Do you get your health insurance through the ACA Exchanges, otherwise known as Obamacare? If so, there's a pretty high chance that your premiums are going to surge starting in January. In some extreme cases, premiums can spike more than 3 times for some payers as Covid-era subsidies are allowed to elapse.
Imagine getting that notification in January - the health insurance you've budgeted, say, $600 a month for instead is $1,200. Or $1,800. Or even $2,000.
That's the reality that many Americans are going to face unless Congress pulls a rabbit out of its hat this week.
Millions of Americans, in red states and blue states alike, are going to be pissed in January. This is not like the normal increase in costs where you're not quite sure what happened. Here, you know it was the federal government. Who are you going to blame?
The party in charge.
| [](https://substack.com/redirect/a5639325-a1b0-46f9-ba0d-a1fd44ce7572?j=eyJ1IjoiMWh2cjYwIn0.VBabVq-VHATXCJqsddI7uMpdiTm0WkVanRDZfvCWMkg)|
---|---|---
Republicans have been trying to find an escape route. Republican Senators' proposal on Thursday for some of the money to go to health savings accounts got 51 votes . . . which is a fail given that you need 60 to overcome a filibuster. The Democrats' proposal of a straightforward extension - the simplest fix - also got 51 votes because a few Republicans jumped on board, including Lisa Murkowski and Dan Sullivan of Alaska and Josh Hawley of Missouri.
The end of health insurance subsidies was contained in the Big Beautiful Bill that passed this summer on a party line vote, so Republicans own this. Democrats forced a government shutdown around this issue, which will come in handy in January. I disliked the shutdown because I thought it would cause pain and disruption without actually accomplishing anything - all the Dems got for a 43-day shutdown was a commitment to vote on healthcare, which just happened. But on the substance and the policy, I thought Republicans had made an egregious error that was going to hurt millions of families and destroy any chance they might have in the '26 midterms.
A few Republicans have wised up to this, particularly moderate House members in vulnerable districts. As I write this, a few moderate members managed to include a vote on an extension of the subsidies attached to a Republican House plan. House Republicans expect this to fail when it's put to a vote but I think it has a chance. There's no guarantee though that, even if the House passes something this week, that the Senate will be able to approve the same legislation before premiums spike for the year on Jan 1. The Republican House plan is much more than just a patch on subsidies; it's 111 pages of modifications to the healthcare industry. That will make Dems very reluctant to vote for it even if includes an extension of benefits.
Rarely have I seen such terrible policy that is going to clearly hurt 20 million-plus Americans so directly and obviously in such a short time frame. The blowback to this will be enormous.
This particular dysfunction is going to have a massive human cost, as at the margins hundreds of thousands of families will forego care. Many are going to drop their insurance coverage - that's a terrible thing. Living without health insurance makes you very vulnerable and also stresses you out for the slightest injury or sickness. People are going to be livid. And they should be.
Our politicians have evaded accountability for years via gerrymandering and feeding their bases on culture war issues. This healthcare debacle is going to be an exception. These aren't indigent voters who will silently get kicked aside - many of the most impacted are middle-class and up. If it doesn't get resolved by a last-minute hail mary - and I doubt it will be - there will be hell to pay for Republicans in the New Year from their own voters. I don't think they have any idea what's coming. Their own base is about to turn on them. Health insurance is about as close to home as it gets for most families. 9 more of their Senators should have flipped and let the Democrats save them from themselves.
My great frustration is that people's well-being is such an afterthought in today's politics. We have real challenges; the people deserve a lot better leadership than this. This time, the cost is all too real.
To reform American politics, check out [Forward](https://substack.com/redirect/9be1df6b-c92d-4b5f-9c80-e173565d23e1?j=eyJ1IjoiMWh2cjYwIn0.VBabVq-VHATXCJqsddI7uMpdiTm0WkVanRDZfvCWMkg), which is having its big year-end [event](https://substack.com/redirect/05e9e1ee-360e-4cfe-90a3-1dd684f2049d?j=eyJ1IjoiMWh2cjYwIn0.VBabVq-VHATXCJqsddI7uMpdiTm0WkVanRDZfvCWMkg) tomorrow. Email [matt@noblemobile.com](https://substack.com/redirect/90aa2ecb-60cd-4517-aa17-96a9b85e7858?j=eyJ1IjoiMWh2cjYwIn0.VBabVq-VHATXCJqsddI7uMpdiTm0WkVanRDZfvCWMkg) if you want a few months off from your wireless bill and want to be rewarded with [Noble Mobile](https://substack.com/redirect/b14a1c84-7f05-4f8b-aa07-3d7e2094db95?j=eyJ1IjoiMWh2cjYwIn0.VBabVq-VHATXCJqsddI7uMpdiTm0WkVanRDZfvCWMkg). [Offline](https://substack.com/redirect/3b263e70-8035-4886-ae0e-08f1b1743003?j=eyJ1IjoiMWh2cjYwIn0.VBabVq-VHATXCJqsddI7uMpdiTm0WkVanRDZfvCWMkg)'s IRL no-phones party is coming to NYC this week. clean_body: Hello, hope that your holiday season is going well. | | ---|---|--- | | | Forwarded this email? ? for more | | ---|---|--- * | | | Dec 15 | | | | | | | | | | | | Hello, hope that your holiday season is going well. Do you get your health insurance through the ACA Exchanges, otherwise known as Obamacare? If so, there's a pretty high chance that your premiums are going to surge starting in January. In some extreme cases, premiums can spike more than 3 times for some payers as Covid-era subsidies are allowed to elapse. Imagine getting that notification in January - the health insurance you've budgeted, say, $600 a month for instead is $1,200. Or $1,800. Or even $2,000. That's the reality that many Americans are going to face unless Congress pulls a rabbit out of its hat this week. Millions of Americans, in red states and blue states alike, are going to be pissed in January. This is not like the normal increase in costs where you're not quite sure what happened. Here, you know it was the federal government. Who are you going to blame? The party in charge. | | ---|---|--- Republicans have been trying to find an escape route. Republican Senators' proposal on Thursday for some of the money to go to health savings accounts got 51 votes . . . which is a fail given that you need 60 to overcome a filibuster. The Democrats' proposal of a straightforward extension - the simplest fix - also got 51 votes because a few Republicans jumped on board, including Lisa Murkowski and Dan Sullivan of Alaska and Josh Hawley of Missouri. The end of health insurance subsidies was contained in the Big Beautiful Bill that passed this summer on a party line vote, so Republicans own this. Democrats forced a government shutdown around this issue, which will come in handy in January. I disliked the shutdown because I thought it would cause pain and disruption without actually accomplishing anything - all the Dems got for a 43-day shutdown was a commitment to vote on healthcare, which just happened. But on the substance and the policy, I thought Republicans had made an egregious error that was going to hurt millions of families and destroy any chance they might have in the '26 midterms. A few Republicans have wised up to this, particularly moderate House members in vulnerable districts. As I write this, a few moderate members managed to include a vote on an extension of the subsidies attached to a Republican House plan. House Republicans expect this to fail when it's put to a vote but I think it has a chance. There's no guarantee though that, even if the House passes something this week, that the Senate will be able to approve the same legislation before premiums spike for the year on Jan 1. The Republican House plan is much more than just a patch on subsidies; it's 111 pages of modifications to the healthcare industry. That will make Dems very reluctant to vote for it even if includes an extension of benefits. Rarely have I seen such terrible policy that is going to clearly hurt 20 million-plus Americans so directly and obviously in such a short time frame. The blowback to this will be enormous. This particular dysfunction is going to have a massive human cost, as at the margins hundreds of thousands of families will forego care. Many are going to drop their insurance coverage - that's a terrible thing. Living without health insurance makes you very vulnerable and also stresses you out for the slightest injury or sickness. People are going to be livid. And they should be. Our politicians have evaded accountability for years via gerrymandering and feeding their bases on culture war issues. This healthcare debacle is going to be an exception. These aren't indigent voters who will silently get kicked aside - many of the most impacted are middle-class and up. If it doesn't get resolved by a last-minute hail mary - and I doubt it will be - there will be hell to pay for Republicans in the New Year from their own voters. I don't think they have any idea what's coming. Their own base is about to turn on them. Health insurance is about as close to home as it gets for most families. 9 more of their Senators should have flipped and let the Democrats save them from themselves. My great frustration is that people's well-being is such an afterthought in today's politics. We have real challenges; the people deserve a lot better leadership than this. This time, the cost is all too real. To reform American politics, check out , which is having its big year-end tomorrow. Email if you want a few months off from your wireless bill and want to be rewarded with . IRL no-phones party is coming to NYC this week. My new comes out in February as a pick-me-up; use code "UBIUBI" for 25% off. Remember to look up and touch grass for the holidays. | | ---|---|--- | | | | | | | (C) 2025 Andrew Yang 111 Sutter Street, 7th Floor, San Francisco, CA 94104 ? ?

Defend our Courts helped land the knockout blow in that race. But instead of retreating in disgrace, MAGA is panicking and unleashing a sinister, desperate plot to cling to power, voters be damned.

These courts are the last line of defense. They are the only bulwark standing between us and MAGA's most twisted fantasies: total abortion bans, unchecked corporate greed, rigged elections.
We stopped them in PA. Now, we have to do it again in AZ, GA, and MI. But here's the cold, hard truth: This fight is going to cost 3x more. The map is bigger, MAGA is more desperate, and the stakes are existential for Democrats,

body:

# We're Building Something Real -- Join Us
### About 70¢ a week to support journalism that's breaking real news.
| | | [Cliff Schecter](https://substack.com/@cliffsedge) and [Blue Amp](https://substack.com/@blueamp)
---
| Dec 15
---
| [](https://substack.com/@cliffsedge)| | [](https://substack.com/@blueamp)
---|---|---
| [](https://substack.com/redirect/a01b5e26-aec9-4a24-854f-f17f14ed1a4e?j=eyJ1IjoiMWh2cjYwIn0.VBabVq-VHATXCJqsddI7uMpdiTm0WkVanRDZfvCWMkg)|
---|---|---
Friends,
I want to be direct with you.
Blue Amp is no longer just an idea or a project -- it's becoming a **real media operation** , and it's happening faster than we expected.
[Join now and save 55% on any membership](https://substack.com/redirect/a01b5e26-aec9-4a24-854f-f17f14ed1a4e?j=eyJ1IjoiMWh2cjYwIn0.VBabVq-VHATXCJqsddI7uMpdiTm0WkVanRDZfvCWMkg)
In the last few weeks alone:
* We **broke a major national story** , publishing documents that show the U.S. Navy ordered the deletion of video after a deadly strike
👉 [https://www.blueamp.co/p/exclusive-us-navy-deleted-video-of](https://substack.com/redirect/d0eb2554-9188-4148-884c-be7e770a2304?j=eyJ1IjoiMWh2cjYwIn0.VBabVq-VHATXCJqsddI7uMpdiTm0WkVanRDZfvCWMkg)
* We've **added serious people** to build this the right way:
* Ellie Leonard, Contributing Editor
* Lawrence Winnerman, Chief Operating Officer
* Dana DuBois, Director of Editorial & Booking
* Melissa Corrigan, Director of Social Strategy & Engagement

These aren't symbolic moves. They're the foundation of something built to last -- especially heading into **2026** , when independent, fearless journalism will matter more than ever.
To support that growth, we're opening up a **limited 55% membership offer** -- privately -- to readers who've shown they care about this work.
### **[That brings membership to $35.98 for the year-- about 70¢ a week.](https://substack.com/redirect/a01b5e26-aec9-4a24-854f-f17f14ed1a4e?j=eyJ1IjoiMWh2cjYwIn0.VBabVq-VHATXCJqsddI7uMpdiTm0WkVanRDZfvCWMkg)**

This isn't about perks.
It's about backing journalism that's willing to dig, publish, and stand its ground -- and helping us scale it responsibly.
If Blue Amp has become part of how you stay informed, grounded, and clear-eyed about what's happening in this country, this is the moment to step inside and support the work.
We're building something real.
We're investing in people and reporting.
And we're grateful to everyone who decides to help carry it forward.
👉 **[Join for About 70¢ a Week -- 55% Off](https://substack.com/redirect/a01b5e26-aec9-4a24-854f-f17f14ed1a4e?j=eyJ1IjoiMWh2cjYwIn0.VBabVq-VHATXCJqsddI7uMpdiTm0WkVanRDZfvCWMkg)**
-- Cliff
**P.S.** This offer is time-limited. Once it closes, we move forward focused on the work ahead and on building toward 2026.

name: Leader, USO Canine Volunteer
subject: Give double the joy
body:
Don't let our troops be forgotten this holiday season.
[](https://www.uso.org)
[MATCH MY GIFT](https://links.e.uso.org/els/v2/LaXVHg_Pmwt43/UXRHUyt5dGdXQ3Y2SkdWRkp1WmphdkoyOEpJWE1aT2M0OW5Xa21wMXk0WXRIalJMa2h5ZEloMHV6R2oydzJuQ1BValFMdEVBNzUxN1pqeU5jMC9YM0M3OVBMcnVqOXpjaGRHdTg5MFJMeHc9S0/ckZ6TW5iZEhlRnFYSlZTOUxFNW5UMnFiN2F6ZTFNRGxHRjVxWkUxMW0yN2pHcUZVNHJRRzFMcHhISE1oaDBDaGpSVU5CUWgvZDcrTks3dG1hVEQxeEJ6bFVhTVY3L0xBS0)
---
[](https://links.e.uso.)
---

[](https://links.e.uso.)
During the holidays, nothing brings the comfort of home like the tail-wagging, sweet love of a dog.
**[If you have someone left on your Christmas list, please consider dedicating the gift of canine companionship for our service members in their name.](https://links.e.uso.org/els/v2/6jKZHV46ZyB-/UXRHUyt5dGdXQ3Y2SkdWRkp1WmphdkoyOEpJWE1aT2M0OW5Xa21wMXk0WXRIalJMa2h5ZEloMHV6R2oydzJuQ1BValFMdEVBNzUxN1pqeU5jMC9YM0M3OVBMcnVqOXpjaGRHdTg5MFJMeHc9S0/ckZ6TW5iZEhlRnFYSlZTOUxFNW5UMnFiN2F6ZTFNRGxHRjVxWkUxMW0yN2pHcUZVNHJRRzFMcHhISE1oaDBDaGpSVU5CUWgvZDcrTks3dG1hVEQxeEJ6bFVhTVY3L0xBS0)**
**This is an especially important program at this time of year. More than 200,000 service members are deployed during the holidays, and they face higher rates of loneliness and isolation. That also means there are 200,000 families missing their loved ones who are serving.**
[](https://links.e.uso.)
---
**[Remember, thanks to the generosity of our friends at FedEx, all gifts will be matched dollar for dollar, up to $100,000.](https://links.e.uso.org/els/v2/6jKZHV46ZyB-/UXRHUyt5dGdXQ3Y2SkdWRkp1WmphdkoyOEpJWE1aT2M0OW5Xa21wMXk0WXRIalJMa2h5ZEloMHV6R2oydzJuQ1BValFMdEVBNzUxN1pqeU5jMC9YM0M3OVBMcnVqOXpjaGRHdTg5MFJMeHc9S0/ckZ6TW5iZEhlRnFYSlZTOUxFNW5UMnFiN2F6ZTFNRGxHRjVxWkUxMW0yN2pHcUZVNHJRRzFMcHhISE1oaDBDaGpSVU5CUWgvZDcrTks3dG1hVEQxeEJ6bFVhTVY3L0xBS0)**
**[MATCH MY GIFT](https://links.e.uso.org/els/v2/YWdETYq~ZpQN/UXRHUyt5dGdXQ3Y2SkdWRkp1WmphdkoyOEpJWE1aT2M0OW5Xa21wMXk0WXRIalJMa2h5ZEloMHV6R2oydzJuQ1BValFMdEVBNzUxN1pqeU5jMC9YM0M3OVBMcnVqOXpjaGRHdTg5MFJMeHc9S0/ckZ6TW5iZEhlRnFYSlZTOUxFNW5UMnFiN2F6ZTFNRGxHRjVxWkUxMW0yN2pHcUZVNHJRRzFMcHhISE1oaDBDaGpSVU5CUWgvZDcrTks3dG1hVEQxeEJ6bFVhTVY3L0xBS0)**
---
Please rush your Christmas gift today, while it will be matched, to help bring the comfort and love of a therapy dog and other critical support to our heroes who have given so much for us.
On behalf of our troops,
The USO
FedEx is matching all gifts to the USO up to a total of $100,000, through December 31, 2025. Your contribution will be used where it is needed most.

name: Pete Buttigieg
subject: Please, join me in supporting Deb Haaland
disclaimer_text: PAID FOR AND AUTHORIZED BY DEB FOR NEW MEXICO**
body:
It’s Pete Buttigieg, reaching out on behalf of my friend Deb Haaland.

It’s Pete Buttigieg, reaching out on behalf of my friend Deb Haaland.
| | |
---
[](https://act.debhaaland.com/go/2453?t=1&akid=6237%2E400544%2EXRqa6D)
Folks, it’s Pete Buttigieg.
After years serving alongside Deb Haaland in the Cabinet – and getting to know her as both a colleague and a friend – I can say with real confidence that she is exactly the kind of leader New Mexico needs. I’m proud to endorse her to be the state’s next governor.
[**Will you join me in supporting Deb by making a $5 contribution to help power her campaign?**](https://act.debhaaland.com/go/2475?t=2&akid=6237%2E400544%2EXRqa6D)
_If you've saved payment information with ActBlue Express, your donation will go through immediately:_ | | | [Donate $10 >>](https://act.debhaaland.com/go/2455?t=3&akid=6237%2E400544%2EXRqa6D)
---
| | [Donate $35 >>](https://act.debhaaland.com/go/2456?t=4&akid=6237%2E400544%2EXRqa6D)
---
| | [Donate $50 >>](https://act.debhaaland.com/go/2457?t=5&akid=6237%2E400544%2EXRqa6D)
---
| | [Other Amount >>](https://act.debhaaland.com/go/2453?t=6&akid=6237%2E400544%2EXRqa6D)
---
Deb brings a rare combination of groundedness and vision to public service. I saw it up close:
As a member of Congress and as Secretary of the Interior, she fought for working families – helping small businesses open their doors, expanding clean-energy job opportunities, and delivering essential water and infrastructure projects to rural communities.
Our departments partnered to expand transportation access on public lands. Thanks in part to her leadership, we secured major investments like the clean-energy shuttle fleet at the Grand Canyon – an example of the results that can come from the forward-looking, practical approach she brings to her work.
And Deb’s leadership is shaped by lived experience. She knows what it means to navigate affordability challenges, to raise a child as a single mother, to rely on public programs like food stamps and WIC, and to stitch together multiple jobs – including running a small business – to make ends meet. Her understanding of what families are up against is not theoretical. It’s personal. **And that’s exactly the kind of perspective that makes for an effective governor.**
New Mexico deserves a governor who will deliver for working families from day one. But the governorship has flipped parties every eight years, and national Republicans see this as their next opportunity to install another Trump-aligned governor. The stakes are not small.
[**This will be a hard-fought race, folks. That’s why I’m committed to helping elect Deb as New Mexico’s next governor – and why I’m asking you to join me. If you can, will you chip in $5 or whatever amount feels right to support her campaign?**](https://act.debhaaland.com/go/2453?t=7&akid=6237%2E400544%2EXRqa6D)
| | [Donate](https://act.debhaaland.com/go/2453?t=8&akid=6237%2E400544%2EXRqa6D)
---
Thank you for your engagement and your support,

Pete
|
* * *

---
| | [](https://act.debhaaland.com/go/1102?t=10&akid=6237%2E400544%2EXRqa6D)

name: Brad Finstad
subject: My new bill could expose massive SNAP fraud in Minnesota
disclaimer_text: Paid for by Finstad for Congress
body:
| | | | |
---
| **Friend,** This week, I introduced the **SNAP Data Transparency and Oversight Act** : a bill that would finally force states to provide beneficiary-level Supplemental Nutrition Assistance Program (SNAP) data to the U.S. Department of Agriculture (USDA) when asked... or lose federal funding. I didn’t do this lightly. I did it because the stakes are massive. Here’s the truth:
Minnesota refused to provide the USDA the basic data needed to prevent fraud. Instead of cooperating with oversight, the Walz administration **took legal action to block federal review.**
USDA officials have already uncovered stunning abuse in the states that have complied:
* **186,000 dead participants still receiving benefits**
* **500,000 individuals receiving benefits twice**
* **Transactions exceeding $10,000**
* **Nearly half a million questionable eligibility cases under scrutiny**

Meanwhile, Minnesota, a state that spent over **$850 million in federal taxpayer dollars on SNAP** **last year alone** , won’t turn over the data that would reveal exactly how much fraud is occurring here at home.
And this comes on the heels of more than $1 billion stolen across multiple fraud schemes in our state. Minnesotans are tired of the excuses. They want accountability. They want transparency. And they deserve a government that safeguards their tax dollars instead of looking the other way.
That’s why I’m leading this fight.
But let me be honest with you: every time we push for oversight and integrity, the Left attacks us. They’d rather pretend the system works perfectly than admit fraud and mismanagement have become rampant under their watch. Your support is what allows me to stand firm.
If you believe taxpayers deserve answers and that Minnesota should not be a sanctuary for fraud, I need you with me.
**Please chip in today to help me continue fighting for transparency, accountability, and the responsible use of your hard-earned money.**
---
| | [Donate Here →](http://link.finstadforcongress.com/api/LinkHandler/getaction2?%3d)
---
| This is about protecting families who genuinely need help and safeguarding the trust of every Minnesota taxpayer.
Thanks for standing with me.
— Brad

body:
Hochul’s actions speak louder than words...
Hochul’s actions speak louder than words...
[View in Web Browser](https://link.email.america1stwarroom.com/click/e1156094/VaHR0cHM6Ly93cm8ubnVjbGV1c2VtYWlsLmNvbS9hbXBsaWZ5L3YvREJybnNBYjM4Ug/SZW1haWxfYW1lcmljYU9ORXN0d2Fycm9vbV9jb20yNTEyMTU/LbmNsYW9CU2dlZA/qP2hpZHM9bmNyR0JDZVhiVDlSMGVscw/gaUAZzA/s6jd9fc0909)

| [](https://link.email.america1stwarroom.)
---
Patriot,
---
Kathy Hochul is still _saying_ that she’ll comfortably win the governor’s race.
---
But actions speak louder than words – _and behind the scenes, Hochul is _acting_ like a candidate running in one of 2026’s **TOP battleground races**_!
---
Her latest move is bringing on veteran Democrat operatives who have worked on other recent top battleground races – including Michigan, Georgia, and Wisconsin – in a desperate bid to boost her struggling reelection campaign.
---
Yes, you read that right... The Democrats are so threatened by our grassroots campaign, that they’re now treating New York state like _Michigan, Georgia, and Wisconsin_!
---
**_New York Post_ : “Hochul beefs up campaign staff with battleground political veterans in sign of looming GOP threat”**
“Gov. Kathy Hochul is filling the upper echelons of her re-election team with alumni of campaigns in battleground states and other recent high-stakes political fights – a signal she may be **_expecting a tough race_** even in deep-blue New York.”
---
This PROVES what I’ve been saying all along: No state has moved further to the right in recent years than New York!
---
Forget all the conventional wisdom about New York being a “blue” state...
---
**With Hochul at the top of the ticket, New York is a TOP 2026 battleground – and like the polling shows, the governor’s race is currently a _TOSS-UP_. **
---
But winning a toss-up race like this means leaving no stone unturned.
---
_That starts with raising the funds needed to reach every last voter, ensure our message isn’t drowned out by the Democrats’ multimillion-dollar ad buys, and build a voter turnout operation that can compete with the Left’s Obama-funded ballot-harvesting machine._
---
With our campaign’s FIRST and ONLY end-of-year deadline rapidly approaching, we need to keep our foot on the gas to ensure we CRUSH our goal.
---
_Patriot, as one of my top grassroots supporters who’s ALWAYS had my back when I needed it most, can I count on _YOU_ to stand with me in the fight to FIRE Hochul at this pivotal moment for our campaign? _
---
[**Please make a grassroots contribution to help me WIN what is rapidly becoming the TOP battleground race in the entire country for the midterms – and help unlock a 600% MATCH.**](https://link.email.america1stwarroom.)
---

Elise Stefanik

subject: Sign On: Expel Randy Fine from Congress
disclaimer_text: PAID FOR BY ILHAN FOR CONGRESS
body:
Far-right GOP Congressman Randy Fine really said this
# [](https://act.ilhanomar.com/go/35?t=1&akid=1998%2E791427%2EsrDTqy)
[](https://act.ilhanomar.com/signup/em-dec25-expel-fine/?source=em251215-1-180&akid=1998%2E791427%2EsrDTqy)
No, this isn’t a “hot mic” moment.
This isn’t a mistake, or a misunderstanding of words.
Far-right GOP Congressman Randy Fine really said this. And he was so proud of it that he tweeted it out, too.
**Randy Fine is a disgusting, violent racist who is unfit for office. He should be removed from Congress.**
**[Add your name to Ilhan Omar’s petition calling for Randy Fine to be expelled from office immediately >>**](https://act.ilhanomar.com/signup/em-dec25-expel-fine/?source=em251215-1-180&akid=1998%2E791427%2EsrDTqy)
**[Sign on now](https://act.ilhanomar.com/signup/em-dec25-expel-fine/?source=em251215-1-180&akid=1998%2E791427%2EsrDTqy)**
This isn’t the first time Randy Fine has shown his racist contempt for Muslims and Arabs.
He has called Ilhan a “Muslim terrorist” and issued a thinly-veiled death threat.
He’s moved to try to denaturalize Zohran Mamdani, and has threatened the same towards Ilhan.
At the height of Israel’s blockade of humanitarian aid into Gaza, he replied to a report of deaths from malnutrition with an image of hungry Gazans by saying “starve away.”
And a few months ago on Fox News, Randy suggested that Israel should drop a nuclear bomb on Gaza to resolve the conflict, killing millions of innocent civilians.
Now, he lets the mask slip fully off. He thinks ‘mainstream Muslims’ need to be ‘destroyed.’ It’s a clearly and plainly genocidal statement.
**Randy Fine is simply a despicable, pathetic man. Americans of all parties and of all backgrounds should not and will not stand for his violent rhetoric. It’s time for him to go.**
**[Add your name to Ilhan’s petition calling for Randy Fine to be expelled from Congress. Every signature helps us send a message that Americans are seeing the hatred being spewed, and are ready to fight back against it.**](https://act.ilhanomar.com/signup/em-dec25-expel-fine/?source=em251215-1-180&akid=1998%2E791427%2EsrDTqy)
**[ADD YOUR NAME](https://act.ilhanomar.com/signup/em-dec25-expel-fine/?source=em251215-1-180&akid=1998%2E791427%2EsrDTqy)**
In solidarity,
Team Ilhan

# ["We Don't Deport Veterans?" Then Come Look at This Case.](https://substack.com/app-link/post?publication_id=3260111&post_id=181102980&utm_source=post-email-title&utm_campaign=email-post-title&isFreemail=true&r=1hvr60&token=eyJ1c2VyX2lkIjo5MDUwMTE5MiwicG9zdF9pZCI6MTgxMTAyOTgwLCJpYXQiOjE3NjU4MDk2MjgsImV4cCI6MTc2ODQwMTYyOCwiaXNzIjoicHViLTMyNjAxMTEiLCJzdWIiOiJwb3N0LXJlYWN0aW9uIn0.1eBLr9kjHzmI0z7yk8Bn7bAbT3FcPac8HDisRSReAFM)
### Zahid Chaudhry's federal habeas hearing is set. Public presence matters. Congressional action matters. You can be part of both.
| | [Melissa Chaudhry](https://substack.com/@melissa4congress)
---
| Dec 15
---
| [](https://substack.com/@melissa4congress)
---

---
| | | [](https://substack.com/app-link/post?publication_id=3260111&post_id=181102980&utm_source=substack&isFreemail=true&submitLike=true&token=eyJ1c2VyX2lkIjo5MDUwMTE5MiwicG9zdF9pZCI6MTgxMTAyOTgwLCJyZWFjdGlvbiI6IuKdpCIsImlhdCI6MTc2NTgwOTYyOCwiZXhwIjoxNzY4NDAxNjI4LCJpc3MiOiJwdWItMzI2MDExMSIsInN1YiI6InJlYWN0aW9uIn0.nd3Z7xY9PjmhCyfxc5yDZs5aRGY9f3FANK4nEhr5Mo0&utm_medium=email&utm_campaign=email-reaction&r=1hvr60)

| | [](https://substack.com/app-link/post?publication_id=3260111&post_id=181102980&utm_source=substack&utm_medium=email&isFreemail=true&comments=true&token=eyJ1c2VyX2lkIjo5MDUwMTE5MiwicG9zdF9pZCI6MTgxMTAyOTgwLCJpYXQiOjE3NjU4MDk2MjgsImV4cCI6MTc2ODQwMTYyOCwiaXNzIjoicHViLTMyNjAxMTEiLCJzdWIiOiJwb3N0LXJlYWN0aW9uIn0.1eBLr9kjHzmI0z7yk8Bn7bAbT3FcPac8HDisRSReAFM&r=1hvr60&utm_campaign=email-half-magic-comments&action=post-comment&utm_source=substack&utm_medium=email)

| | [READ IN APP](https://open.substack.com/pub/melissa4congress/p/we-dont-deport-veterans-then-come?utm_source=email&redirect=app-store&utm_campaign=email-read-in-app)

On **Monday, December 22nd at 10:00am** , a federal judge in the Western District of Washington will hear my husband Zahid's habeas petition.
This hearing matters -- not just for our family, but for the integrity of the claims being made by those in power about who we are as a country.
Zahid Chaudhry is not an abstraction.
He has lived in the United States for **25 years**.
He has **never broken a single U.S. law**.
He is a **decorated, honorably discharged U.S. Army veteran** , permanently disabled in the course of his service, and **uses a wheelchair**.
He is a **dedicated public servant** , including service as a **sitting officer of the State of Washington**.
He is a **loving husband** and the **father of U.S. citizen children** , including **two babies under the age of three**.
And today, he is imprisoned by ICE.
### **Come Witness the Hearing**
If you are able, I am asking you to come.
Not to protest.
Not to disrupt.
Simply to **sit in the room and bear witness**.
**📍 Hearing Details**
**Date:** Monday, December 22, 2025
**Time:** 10:00 AM
**Courtroom:** Courtroom B
**Court:** Western District of Washington, Federal Court
**Address:** 1717 Pacific Ave, Tacoma, WA
Federal courts are solemn places. They are also human places. Judges notice who is present. They notice when a case is not being quietly buried. Community presence matters -- especially in a case that sits at the crossroads of constitutional law, veterans' rights, disability justice, and family unity.
You do not need legal expertise.
You do not need to speak.
Your presence alone carries weight.
### **The Reality Behind the Rhetoric**
Recently, **DHS Secretary Kristi Noem** has been publicly asserting -- including in Congressional hearings --
> "We don't deport veterans."
> "We've never deported veterans."
Those statements are **not true**.
Veterans _have_ been deported.
Veterans _are_ being deported.
And a **decorated, disabled Army veteran** is currently detained in Washington State, fighting for his freedom and his family **right now**.
That veteran is my husband.
This is not a theoretical policy debate. It is a concrete, documented contradiction between public statements and lived reality.
### **What This Is Doing to Our Family**
Zahid is not just a veteran. He is the emotional center of our household.
Our toddlers are too young to understand why their father is gone -- only that he is missing. Our home has become quieter, heavier, disoriented. Ordinary moments -- bedtime, meals, laughter -- now carry a persistent absence. I am raising two very small children alone while fighting, every day, to bring their father home.
No family should endure this -- least of all the family of a man who gave his body and health to this country.
### **The Call to Action: Make This Impossible to Ignore**
I am asking everyone who supports Zahid -- and especially **leaders of organizations, faith communities, veteran groups, unions, and advocacy networks** -- to act **now**.
📞 **Call Senator Patty Murray: (206) 553-5545**
📞 **Call Senator Maria Cantwell: (206) 220-6600**
And yes -- also contact your **[U.S. Representative](https://substack.com/redirect/5f93a07f-c331-493d-9bfb-ce99e59101d9?j=eyJ1IjoiMWh2cjYwIn0.VBabVq-VHATXCJqsddI7uMpdiTm0WkVanRDZfvCWMkg)** and your **[state legislators](https://substack.com/redirect/22f06002-021c-4133-835b-3f4153a2a520?j=eyJ1IjoiMWh2cjYwIn0.VBabVq-VHATXCJqsddI7uMpdiTm0WkVanRDZfvCWMkg)**.
You can keep it simple:
> "A decorated, disabled U.S. Army veteran named Muhammad Zahid Chaudhry is currently detained by ICE in Washington State. DHS leadership claims they don't deport veterans. That is false. I am asking you to publicly call for his immediate release and for his long-delayed naturalization."
If you want to add one more sentence:
> "Silence in the face of this contradiction makes those public statements meaningless."
That's enough.
Calls matter. Repetition matters. Public pressure matters -- especially when the facts are this clear.
### **Why This Moment Matters**
Courts respond to law.
Elected officials respond to pressure.
Institutions respond when contradictions can no longer be hidden.
Zahid's case brings all of this into the open.
If the claim "we don't deport veterans" is to mean anything at all, then **this case must end** -- with Zahid's release, his naturalization, and accountability for what has been allowed to happen.
If not, then those words are not policy.
They are propaganda.
Please come if you can.
Please call if you can't.
Please share this widely.
Thank you for standing with our family -- and for standing up for truth, dignity, and the basic promise that service to this country should never be repaid with exile.
For integrity; for love --
Melissa

If you would like to get fewer emails, [**_click here._**](https://act.fightforreform.org/go/59469?t=1&akid=23728%2E4449941%2EY6belY) If you would like to unsubscribe, [**_click here._**](https://act.fightforreform.org/go/59470?t=2&akid=23728%2E4449941%2EY6belY) Team, we’re writing to you with a heavy heart today.

[**We’re devastated — truly heartbroken — because something deeply WRONG is happening in our country.**](https://act.fightforreform.org/go/71011?t=3&akid=23728%2E4449941%2EY6belY)

In 2024, Donald Trump was convicted on 34 felony counts...
Yet he still became President.

Now he’s selling out our government to Dark Money billionaires, cozying up to war criminals, and tightening his grip on Washington.

But top Democrats are fighting back with the _No Kings Act_ — a historic bill that would strip Donald Trump of immunity once and for all!

| | Dorian Gray glanced at the picture, and suddenly an uncontrollable feeling of hatred for Basil Hallward came over him, as though it had been suggested to him by the image on the canvas, whispered into his ear by those grinning lips. The mad passions of a hunted animal stirred within him, and he loathed the man who was seated at the table, more than in his whole life he had ever loathed anything. He glanced wildly around. Something glimmered on the top of the painted chest that faced him. His eye fell on it. He knew what it was. It was a knife that he had brought up, some days before, to cut a piece of cord, and had forgotten to take away with him. He moved slowly towards it, passing Hallward as he did so. As soon as he got behind him, he seized it and turned round. Hallward stirred in his chair as if he was going to rise. He rushed at him and dug the knife into the great vein that is behind the ear, crushing the man’s head down on the table and stabbing again and again. There was a stifled groan and the horrible sound of some one choking with blood. Three times the outstretched arms shot up convulsively, waving grotesque, stiff-fingered hands in the air. He stabbed him twice more, but the man did not move. Something began to trickle on the floor. He waited for a moment, still pressing the head down. Then he threw the knife on the table, and listened. He could hear nothing, but the drip, drip on the threadbare carpet. He opened the door and went out on the landing. The house was absolutely quiet. No one was about. For a few seconds he stood bending over the balustrade and peering down into the black seething well of darkness. Then he took out the key and returned to the room, locking himself in as he did so. The thing was still seated in the chair, straining over the table with bowed head, and humped back, and long fantastic arms. Had it not been for the red jagged tear in the neck and the clotted black pool that was slowly widening on the table, one would have said that the man was simply asleep. How quickly it had all been done! He felt strangely calm, and walking over to the window, opened it and stepped out on the balcony. The wind had blown the fog away, and the sky was like a monstrous peacock’s tail, starred with myriads of golden eyes. He looked down and saw the policeman going his rounds and flashing the long beam of his lantern on the doors of the silent houses. The crimson spot of a prowling hansom gleamed at the corner and then vanished. A woman in a fluttering shawl was creeping slowly by the railings, staggering as she went. Now and then she stopped and peered back. Once, she began to sing in a hoarse voice. The policeman strolled over and said something to her. She stumbled away, laughing. A bitter blast swept across the square. The gas-lamps flickered and became blue, and the leafless trees shook their black iron branches to and fro. He shivered and went back, closing the window behind him.
---
